title: Partial — make everything optional
instruction:
`Partial<T>` makes all properties optional. Perfect for update/patch operations where only changed fields are sent.

Use Partial for a settings update function.
successMessage: `Partial<UserSettings>` allows passing any subset of fields. The spread merges with defaults. This is the PATCH endpoint pattern — send only what changed.

title: Required — all fields mandatory
instruction:
`Required<T>` makes all optional properties required. Use it when a function needs all fields to be present.

Build a form submission handler that requires all fields.
successMessage: `Required<SignupForm>` ensures every field is present when the form is submitted. The type transformation makes it explicit that submission needs everything, while the form might be partially filled.

title: Readonly — prevent mutation
instruction:
`Readonly<T>` makes all properties readonly. Useful for frozen configuration and immutable data.

Create readonly configuration objects.
successMessage: `Readonly<T>` prevents accidental mutation. Passing config as `Readonly<T>` signals to functions that they should not modify it — and TypeScript enforces it.

title: Pick — select specific fields
instruction:
`Pick<T, K>` creates a type with only the specified keys. Perfect for API responses that should expose only certain fields.

Create safe public-facing types.
successMessage: `Pick<User, "id" | "name" | "role">` is a type that literally cannot contain `passwordHash`. The security benefit is enforced by the type system — accidental inclusion is a compile error.

title: Omit — remove specific fields
instruction:
`Omit<T, K>` creates a type with all fields except the specified ones. Cleaner than `Pick` when you want to keep most fields.

Omit sensitive or internal fields.
expectedOutput: Fields: id, name, email, role, createdAt, updatedAt
successMessage: `Omit` is cleaner than `Pick` when keeping most fields. `CreateUserInput` removes auto-generated fields — what you'd pass to a POST endpoint. `SafeUser` removes sensitive fields — what you'd return from a GET endpoint.

title: Record — typed key-value maps
instruction:
`Record<K, V>` creates an object type with keys of type `K` and values of type `V`. It's exhaustive when `K` is a union type.

Build an exhaustive lookup table.
successMessage: `Record<Lang, ...>` requires an entry for EVERY lang in the union. Add `"rust"` to `Lang` and TypeScript immediately tells you to add its info. Zero chance of a missing entry.

title: ReturnType — extract function return type
instruction:
`ReturnType<T>` extracts the return type from a function type. Use it to derive types from existing functions without duplication.

Derive types from existing functions.
successMessage: `ReturnType<typeof createUser>` automatically matches whatever `createUser` returns. If you add a field to the return object, `User` updates automatically everywhere it's used.

title: Parameters — extract function parameter types
instruction:
`Parameters<T>` extracts the parameter types as a tuple. Combine with `ReturnType` to fully describe a function.

Use Parameters for logging and validation.
successMessage: `Parameters<T>` gives you `[number, number, "USD"|"EUR"|"GBP", string]`. The `logPayment` function destructures the args tuple with full type safety. Note: the timestamp in TX-0 will vary.

title: Exclude and Extract — filter union types
instruction:
`Exclude<T, U>` removes types from a union. `Extract<T, U>` keeps only matching types. Both filter union members.

Filter response types and status codes.
successMessage: `Extract` keeps only the matching members. `Exclude` removes them. Inside `if (isSuccess(code))`, TypeScript narrows `code` to `SuccessStatus` — the subset of HTTP codes that are successful.

title: NonNullable — remove null and undefined
instruction:
`NonNullable<T>` removes `null` and `undefined` from a union. Use it when you've already checked for null.

Filter out null values from an array.
successMessage: `NonNullable<T>` removes `null | undefined` from the type. After `filterDefined`, the array is `T[]` not `(T | null | undefined)[]`. The type predicate `v is NonNullable<T>` tells TypeScript the narrowed type.

title: ConstructorParameters and InstanceType
instruction:
`ConstructorParameters<T>` extracts a constructor's parameter types. `InstanceType<T>` gives you the instance type of a class. Both are useful for factory patterns.

Build a generic factory using these utilities.
successMessage: `ConstructorParameters` captures all three constructor parameter types as a tuple. `createService` is fully typed — passing the wrong argument type at any position is a compile error.

title: Combining utility types — real patterns
instruction:
Utility types can be combined to express complex transformations. Here are patterns from real codebases.

Build input and output types for a REST API.
successMessage: Combining `Omit`, `Pick`, `Partial`, and intersection types creates precise API types without duplicating the base interface. Every REST API in TypeScript uses variations of these patterns.
